<!-- topic: security -->

# Security

[edikt:directives:start]: #
- Any drift in the `permissions` block MUST be detected on the next install/upgrade and MUST prompt the user before overwrite. (ref: ADR-017)
- Integrity MUST be tracked via the sidecar mechanism defined by INV-005(b): `~/.edikt/state/settings-managed.json` MUST record the canonical hash of the managed keys. (ref: ADR-017)
- The `permissions.defaultMode` MUST be set to `askBeforeAllow` — any tool not explicitly allowed or denied prompts the user once. (ref: ADR-017)
- The `permissions` block MUST be edikt-managed. (ref: ADR-017)
- User-added top-level keys outside `permissions` MUST survive untouched on install/upgrade. (ref: ADR-017)
- `templates/settings.json.tmpl` MUST ship with an explicit `permissions` block with both `deny` and `allow` lists populated. (ref: ADR-017)
[edikt:directives:end]: #
[edikt:directives:sha256]: # 35668c527768ced3ddf68af9d498ce262c8d73e9d8062991a126e3a6ed59fc87

[edikt:prohibitions:start]: #
## Prohibitions
- MUST NOT ship with no default permissions and link only to a recommended-settings doc — documented-but-unshipped defaults are effectively not the default and leave the HI-9 audit finding open. (ref: ADR-017)
- MUST NOT use a strict allow-list with no deny list — every new tool, every new Bash invocation, and every WebFetch to an unfamiliar domain would prompt, guaranteeing permission fatigue. (ref: ADR-017)
- MUST NOT use allow-by-default with only a narrow deny list — this posture is equivalent to shipping no permissions block and fails to address the HI-9 audit finding. (ref: ADR-017)
[edikt:prohibitions:end]: #
[edikt:prohibitions:sha256]: # bb7e38e8055ca1a854c7b88cb452c8a79853b758613adff1dc9903b4cd41b8db

[edikt:manual:start]: #
[edikt:manual:end]: #
